Limit contact to what's necessary and set firm limits.

faq.q: Should I try to change them?
faq.a: You can't change someone who doesn't want to change. Focus on what you control: your reaction, your limits, the distance you choose.

title: Staying Connected Across Distance
intro: Moving away, studying abroad, traveling — distance tests relationships. With a little effort, they hold up just fine.

sections.heading: Regularity beats length
sections.body: A long-distance friendship doesn't survive on one three-hour catch-up call every six months. It lives on small, regular contact: a message, a meme, a photo, a shared piece of news. That light but continuous thread keeps closeness alive. A short check-in every week beats a long silence followed by a marathon catch-up.

sections.heading: Plan the real moments
sections.body: Video calls and visits don't happen by themselves at a distance — you have to schedule them. Set up a recurring time, a monthly call, a visit planned in advance. Without planning, the intention stays as a vague 'we should catch up sometime' that never materializes. Putting a date on it turns intention into a real moment.

sections.heading: Share your everyday life
sections.body: Distance cuts off the sharing of small things — and that's what creates the most drift. Recreate it: share a detail from your day, tell them about a minor annoyance or a small joy. Those everyday nothings, more than big news, are what keep you feeling like you're still part of each other's lives.

steps: Send small, regular signs rather than rare long messages.
Schedule video calls at a set time.
Plan visits in advance.
Share everyday details, not just major updates.

faq.q: We talk less — is the friendship over?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Real friendships can handle quiet phases and pick back up fast. If the bond is solid, distance puts it on pause — it doesn't end it.

title: Creating More Opportunities to Meet People
intro: You don't meet new people by accident — you have to build the contexts where those encounters become likely.

sections.heading: Go where the people are
sections.body: To meet people, you need to put yourself in social contexts: a team sport, a class, a workshop, volunteering, a club. These settings bring people together around a shared interest, which makes first exchanges dramatically easier. Staying home waiting for it to happen doesn't work — connection is something you create, not something that falls from the sky.

sections.heading: Prioritize recurring activities
sections.body: A one-off event lets you cross paths with people, but a weekly activity lets you see them again — and repetition is what turns an acquaintance into a friend. Prioritize regular activities. You'll see the same faces week after week, familiarity sets in, and connections form almost naturally.

sections.heading: Say yes to invitations
sections.body: A lot of social opportunities die in a reflexive 'nah, I'm tired.' When someone invites you somewhere, say yes more often — even if the enthusiasm isn't immediate. That's where you meet friends-of-friends and expand your circle. Every yes is a door; every systematic no quietly closes your social world.

steps: Sign up for a social activity you're actually interested in.
Prioritize recurring activities over one-off events.
Say yes more often to invitations, even without immediate excitement.
Make moves toward your friends' friends to broaden your circle.

faq.q: What if I don't have time?
faq.a: One regular activity is enough to start. It's not about quantity — it's about consistent presence in a place where connections can grow.

title: Reading Nonverbal Cues
intro: A huge part of communication happens through the body. Learning to read it helps you understand what's really going on.

sections.heading: The body says a lot
sections.body: Posture, gestures, expressions, proximity, tone of voice: all of it completes — and sometimes contradicts — the words. Someone who says 'I'm fine' with a closed-off body and averted eyes is sending two opposing messages. Learning to notice these signals makes you more attentive to what people around you are actually experiencing, beyond what they say.

sections.heading: Read clusters of signals
sections.body: One isolated gesture means nothing: crossed arms might just mean they're cold. The key is to observe coherent sets: crossed arms plus averted eyes plus short responses together suggest discomfort. Look for clusters of signals pointing in the same direction, and compare them to that person's usual behavior.

sections.heading: Verify rather than assume
sections.body: Body language gives you clues, not certainties. You can misread a signal. When something seems off, don't draw your own conclusions — verify with a gentle question: 'everything okay?' Nonverbal cues tell you something might be going on; words tell you what exactly.

steps: Observe posture, eye contact, and tone — not just the words.
Look for coherent clusters of signals, not isolated gestures.
Compare what you see to that person's normal behavior.
Verify your intuition with a question — don't conclude alone.

faq.q: Can you actually detect a lie through body language?
faq.a: No — that's a myth. No body signal reliably proves a lie. Nonverbal cues help you sense discomfort; they don't make you a lie detector.

title: Smiling Naturally and Warmly
intro: A smile is the simplest and most powerful social signal for making others feel at ease.

sections.heading: The power of a genuine smile
sections.body: A smile says 'I'm glad to see you, you're welcome here' before a single word is spoken. It relaxes the other person, opens the exchange, and instantly makes you more approachable. It's one of the most high-value social gestures: it costs nothing and changes the entire feel of an encounter. Someone who smiles naturally is simply easier to approach.

sections.heading: Genuine over forced
sections.body: A forced smile — one that only involves the mouth — is detectable and rings false. A genuine smile also reaches the eyes, which crinkle slightly. To find it, think of something genuinely pleasant, or let it come as a natural reaction to the person rather than plastering it on. Natural always beats performed.

sections.heading: Calibrate to the context
sections.body: Smiling doesn't mean smiling constantly — that can read as strange or nervous. The right dose: a warm smile when you arrive, during pleasant exchanges, when the other person says something positive. Your face should stay alive and expressive, not frozen in a permanent grin. The sincerity of the moment is what counts.

steps: Smile when you arrive and when you greet people.
Let the smile reach your eyes too.
Think of something pleasant to make it genuine.
Keep it natural: an expressive face, not a fixed smile.

faq.q: My smile looks weird to me — what do I do?
faq.a: That's usually self-criticism, not your actual smile. Practice in front of a mirror while thinking of a good memory — a genuine smile relaxes quickly with habit.
